title: Fast Adiabatic Quantum Gates via Hyperfine Intermediate States
authors: Jiayin Fan, Xingdong Zhao, Manqi Zhang, Fangfang Xie, Jing Qian
abstract: The appeal of adiabatic quantum computing lies in its intrinsic robustness against various technical imperfections, making it attractive for many quantum information applications. However, it faces a fundamental challenge: accelerating the adiabatic operations while preserving adiabaticity within the qubit coherence time. In this article, we propose an electromagnetically induced transparency-based adiabatic CNOT gate protocol which harnesses atomic hyperfine intermediate states (HISs) to speed up the adiabatic evolution. The HISs, naturally-existed in two-photon transitions, often need to be suppressed due to their significant decay errors. In contrast, this paper introduces a novel method that utilizes appropriately chosen HISs not only to enhance the adiabaticity in STAY pathway but also to accelerate the population transfer in TRANSFER pathway. Through pulse optimization, we achieve adiabatic gate fidelities exceeding 0.9991 within 0.3903 μs in realistic Cs atomic setups. To demonstrate the generality of protocol we further assess the impact of decays from multiple HIS and extend our model to arbitrary number of states, providing a practical route toward fast and robust adiabatic quantum gates in Rydberg-atom platforms.
summary: This paper proposes an advanced protocol for realizing fast, high-fidelity CNOT gates in Rydberg-atom systems by ingeniously incorporating hyperfine intermediate states. By optimizing pulse sequences across two distinct dynamical pathways (STAY and TRANSFER), the authors significantly accelerate the gate operation time while maintaining fidelity above 0.9991. This represents a practical advancement toward scalable quantum computation using neutral atoms.
main_problem: The core challenge is accelerating adiabatic quantum gate operations while maintaining high fidelity, as standard protocols are inherently too slow.
main_result: The proposed protocol achieves fast and robust CNOT gates with fidelities exceeding 0.9991 in a short time frame ($\sim 0.39 \mu s$).
method: The method involves utilizing hyperfine intermediate states (HISs) within an EIT-based scheme to simultaneously enhance the energy gap (STAY pathway) and accelerate population transfer (TRANSFER pathway).
model_or_system: The system is modeled using Rydberg-atom platforms, involving control and target qubits coupled via electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) pathways that incorporate multiple HISs. The dynamics are governed by non-Hermitian Hamiltonians to account for decay.
key_observables: Gate fidelity ($F_0$, $F_s$, $F_t$), gate time ($	au$), and the energy gap between dark and bright states.
important_parameters: The efficiency depends critically on satisfying a 'k-factor' condition ($k_r = k_{0,1}$), and performance is tested against varying intermediate decay rates ($\gamma_p$).
main_assumptions: Adiabatic elimination of highly detuned HISs is assumed, and the protocol is optimized using pulse shaping techniques (e.g., Genetic Algorithm).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate energy level diagrams showing STAY and TRANSFER pathways, and compare gate performance under ideal vs. non-ideal conditions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of slow adiabatic gates, details the Hamiltonian structure involving HISs for both STAY and TRANSFER dynamics, presents the optimization procedure using pulse shaping, and concludes with quantitative results demonstrating speedup and fidelity enhancement.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the practical limitations of quantum simulation by providing a concrete mechanism (HIS utilization) to reconcile the conflict between gate speed and adiabatic robustness in atomic platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of EIT-based fast adiabatic CNOT gates with HISs. (a) shows the atomic energy levels in e.g., Cs atoms. The control qubit atom is modeled as an effective two-photon system between |1⟩c and |r⟩c with |0⟩c un-excited, which is operated by two separate square-π pulses (labeled Ωr) serving as the control knob. The target qubit forming of an inverted-Y five-level configuration exhibits the excita- tion to |r⟩t from two ground states |0⟩t and |1⟩t mediated by HISs |P1⟩and |P2⟩, detuned by ∆1 and ∆2, respectively. γp,r denote the spontaneous decay rates. See Table I for more de- tailed parameters. (b1,b2) illustrate the STAY and TRANS- FER pathways, corresponding to the...

FIG. 2. Comparison of the target-atom population dynamics in the protocols with double (left, enhanced scheme) and sin- gle (right, original scheme) HISs. Panels (a1,a2) correspond to the STAY pathway and panels (b1,b2) show the TRANSFER pathway in which the final-target-state population presents the pathway fidelity. Numerical results, based on Schr¨odinger equation (with decays γp,r) and analytical dark |d⟩k state re- sults (without decays), are given by solid and dashed curves respectively. In all plots the parameters are adopted from Cs atoms, which are Ωmax p /2π = 80 MHz, Ωc/2π = 300 MHz, ∆1/2π = 1.2 GHz, ∆2/2π = 1.26 GHz, k = 0.775, γr = 1.3 kHz. Here we set a moderate decoherence...

FIG. 3. Average gate fidelity F0 as a function of the interme- diate decay rate γp based on the optimized Bernstein-shaped pulses. Solid curves are obtained via individual optimiza- tions for every γp value (Case III) while dashed curves are calculated by using the fixed optimized pulse at γp = 6.5 MHz (Case I). Inset: Dependence of the derivation ∆F0 = F0(γp0) −F0(γp) on the decay rate γp with γp0 = 6.5 MHz for Case I.

FIG. 4. Gate performance for the adiabaticity-enhanced case (kr = k0,1) and the failure (kr ̸= k0,1) case. (a1,a2) We show the average fidelity F0 over 40 independent optimization runs for two cases and respectively present (b1,b2) the opti- mized Raman pulse amplitude Ωp(t) and (c1-2,d1-2) the two- pathway population dynamics for a specific solution, as high- lighted by red diamonds in (a1) and (a2). Here, the specific- solution parameters are β1∼4/2π = (2.35, 73.51, 75.54, 51.06) MHz which results in τ = 0.2903 µs for the adiabaticity- enhanced case. While β1∼4/2π = (80.00, 78.80, 78.43, 37.54) MHz, τ = 0.1928 µs for the failure case.
